title: Epigenome Editing
focus: Definition and advantages of epigenome editing over traditional gene editing.
rationale: A distinct, highly testable conceptual difference between epigenome editing and traditional DNA-cutting methods.
sectionExcerpt: Epigenome consists of chemical compounds that modify or regulate gene expression. Epigenome editing allows for precise control over gene expression with reversible changes without cutting DNA, making it safer than traditional gene editing.
evidenceBullets: Epigenome regulates gene expression via chemical compounds.
Epigenome editing does not involve cutting DNA.
Changes made via epigenome editing are reversible.

title: GM Crops and Adoption Status in India
focus: Definition of GMOs/Transgenics and the current approval status of specific GM crops in India.
rationale: Core UPSC topic covering the distinction between GMOs and transgenics, and the factual status of Bt Cotton, Bt Brinjal, and GM Mustard.
sectionExcerpt: GM Crops are Genetically Modified Organisms with artificially altered DNA. Transgenics are a subset containing DNA from another species (e.g., Bt Cotton). In India, Bt cotton is the only approved GM crop for cultivation. Bt-Brinjal is under a moratorium, and GM mustard (DMH-11) has conditional approval currently under Supreme Court review.
evidenceBullets: Transgenics contain DNA sequences from another species.
Bt cotton is the only GM crop approved for cultivation in India.
Bt-brinjal is under a moratorium despite GEAC clearance.
GM mustard (DMH-11) conditional approval is under Supreme Court review.

title: Regulatory Framework of GM Crops in India
focus: Key institutions and acts governing GM crops, excluding GEAC.
rationale: Institutional framework questions are common in UPSC. Differentiating the roles of FSSAI, RCGM, SBCC, and DLC is crucial.
sectionExcerpt: The regulatory framework includes the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, which requires FSSAI approval for GM food. The Review Committee on Genetic Manipulation (RCGM) under the DBT monitors R&D. The State Biotechnology Coordination Committee (SBCC) reviews safety in institutions, and the District Level Committee (DLC) inspects and reports compliance.
evidenceBullets: FSSAI approval is mandatory for import, manufacture, use, or sale of GM food.
RCGM operates under the Department of Biotechnology (DBT) to monitor R&D projects.
SBCC reviews safety and control measures for GMOs at the state level.
DLC inspects and reports compliance to SBCC or GEAC.

title: Site Directed Nuclease (SDN) Technology
focus: Classification of SDN technologies (1, 2, 3) and their regulatory approval status in India.
rationale: Emerging biotechnology concept. The distinction between SDN-1, SDN-2, and SDN-3 based on the use of a DNA template is highly examinable.
sectionExcerpt: SDN technology is categorized into three types: SDN-1 (mutagenesis without a DNA template), SDN-2 (mutagenesis using a DNA template), and SDN-3 (insertion of a large DNA sequence using a template). India's biosafety regulations have approved genome editing of SDN-1 and SDN-2 types for general crops.
evidenceBullets: SDN-1 involves site-directed mutagenesis without a DNA sequence template.
SDN-2 uses a DNA sequence template for mutagenesis.
SDN-3 involves the insertion of a gene or large DNA sequence using a template.
SDN-1 and SDN-2 genes are approved under India's biosafety regulations for general crops.

title: Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee (GEAC)
focus: Genesis, nodal ministry, and responsibilities of the GEAC.
rationale: GEAC is the apex body for GM crop approval in India and a frequent subject of UPSC Prelims questions regarding its statutory backing and ministry.
sectionExcerpt: GEAC is a statutory committee constituted under the 1989 Rules framed under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. It operates under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC). It is responsible for appraising proposals for the release of genetically engineered organisms and products, and activities involving large-scale use of hazardous microorganisms.
evidenceBullets: GEAC is a statutory committee under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
It functions under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC).
It appraises proposals for the release of GE organisms and products.
It appraises large-scale use of hazardous microorganisms and recombinants.
